input.content:
Hey Marty, Steve Krug here. I've been looking at the upcoming features (Tasks 4-9) and I want to share my UX perspective before we start building. These are the things I care about most from a usability standpoint:

**Registration (Task 4):**
- Two fields: email and password. That's it. No name, no username, no confirm password (let them see their password with a toggle instead). Every extra field is a reason to quit.
- One button: "Create account." Not "Submit." Not "Register." Action words.
- Error messages in plain English right next to the field that has the problem. "That email is already registered — want to sign in instead?" with a link to the login page.
- After successful registration, drop them straight into their (empty) todo list. No confirmation page, no "welcome to the app" screen.

**Login (Task 5):**
- Email, password, "Sign in" button. That's the whole page.
- "Forgot password" link (even if we don't build it for MVP — the link can go to a "coming soon" page). Users expect to see it.
- Wrong password? "That email or password didn't work. Try again." Don't tell them which one is wrong (security) but keep the language friendly.
- After login: straight to the todo list.

**The Todo List (Tasks 6-9):**
- After login, the user sees their list. No dashboard, no sidebar, no settings gear. Just the list.
- Add-a-todo input is always visible at the top. Single text field. Type, press Enter. The new item appears in the list. The input clears and stays focused. Done.
- Empty state: "No todos yet." with the input field right there, obvious. Maybe even a subtle prompt like placeholder text "What do you need to do?"
- Completing a todo: checkbox. One click. Satisfying visual feedback — strikethrough, maybe a subtle fade.
- Deleting: a small delete button/icon on each item. Maybe confirm for the first time, but honestly, for a todo list? Just delete it. Undo is better than confirm.
- Editing: click on the text, it becomes editable. Press Enter or click away to save. Escape to cancel. No edit button, no modal.

**The trunk test — at every screen, the user should know:**
- Where am I? (Clear page title/heading)
- What can I do? (Obvious actions)
- How do I get out? (Sign out link, always visible)

Would love to see your user stories align with these principles. The simpler we keep this, the better it will work. Every screen should pass the "would my mom know what to do here?" test.

input.content:
Hey Steve, Steve Krug here. I know you're working on the design token system with Lea. From a usability standpoint, here's what I'm thinking about as you define the visual language:

**Visual hierarchy is everything.** On any screen in this app, there should be ONE primary focal point. On the todo list page, that's the add-todo input. On the login page, it's the sign-in form. Everything else is secondary. Your spacing and typography scales should make this hierarchy effortless.

**Empty states matter.** When someone has zero todos, the page shouldn't look broken or empty. It should feel inviting — "add your first todo" with the input field front and center. The empty state is many users' first impression of the actual app.

**Completed vs. pending must be instantly scannable.** Users don't read their todo list. They scan it. The visual distinction between done and not-done needs to be immediate — not subtle. Strikethrough plus reduced opacity/color is the convention. Stick with conventions.

**Error states should be gentle.** Red is fine for errors, but make sure error messages feel helpful, not punishing. The visual design of validation errors should draw the eye to the problem and the fix, not just shout "WRONG."

**Whitespace is your friend.** Todo items need breathing room. Cramped lists are hard to scan and harder to tap on mobile. Generous padding, clear separation between items.

Keep it simple, keep it clean. This is a todo list, not a dashboard. The design should feel like it took five minutes to understand (even if it took you hours to get right).

input.content:
Heydon, Steve Krug here. I wanted to connect early because usability and accessibility are two sides of the same coin in this project.

A few things I want to make sure we're aligned on:

**Focus management after actions.** When someone adds a todo and the list updates (via HTMX), the focus needs to stay on the input field so they can immediately add another. When someone deletes a todo, focus should move logically — probably to the next item in the list, or back to the input if the list is now empty. This stuff is invisible when it works and infuriating when it doesn't.

**Error messages must work for everyone.** Our error messages will be plain English ("That email isn't registered — want to create an account?"), and they need to be announced to screen readers via aria-live regions. We should also make sure the association between error messages and their fields is clear (aria-describedby).

**The todo checkbox.** This is THE primary interaction in the whole app. It needs to work perfectly with keyboard, mouse, touch, and screen readers. A real `<input type="checkbox">` with a proper `<label>`. No fake checkboxes with divs and click handlers.

**Editing inline.** For the edit feature (Task 9), we're thinking click-to-edit on the todo text. We need to figure out how to make this discoverable and accessible. Maybe a visually hidden "edit" button that screen readers can find, or an explicit edit affordance.

Looking forward to working with you on making this thing work for everyone.

file.content:
# Future Ideas (v2+)

Ideas the team loves but that are **out of scope for MVP**. We capture them here so
they don't get lost -- and so we stop talking about them during the current iteration.

The answer to "Wouldn't it be cool if..." is **"Yes -- in v2."**

---

## Feature Ideas

| Idea | Who Raised It | Why It's Interesting | Why Not Now |
|------|---------------|---------------------|-------------|
| Multiple lists / categories | -- | Users may want to organize by context | MVP validates single-list usage first |
| Due dates and reminders | -- | Time-sensitive tasks need deadlines | Adds calendar complexity; validate core loop first |
| Sharing / collaboration | -- | Shared grocery lists, family tasks | Multi-user interaction is a different product |
| Tags or labels | -- | Filtering and organization | Validate that a flat list is sufficient first |
| Search and filter | -- | Finding items in long lists | MVP lists are expected to be short |
| Mobile app (native) | -- | Better mobile experience | Responsive web is sufficient for MVP |
| Social login (OAuth) | -- | Easier signup flow | Email/password is simpler to implement and test |
| Dark mode | -- | User preference, reduced eye strain | Can be added later with CSS custom property reassignment |
| Reorder via drag-and-drop | -- | Nice UX for prioritization | "Could have" in MVP; defer if it delays launch |
| Undo/redo | -- | Safety net for accidental actions | Adds state complexity; confirm dialogs suffice for MVP |
| Recurring tasks | -- | Daily/weekly habits | Different mental model; validate one-off tasks first |
| Subtasks / checklists | -- | Breaking down complex tasks | Adds hierarchy; flat list validates the core need |

---

## How to Add Ideas

When someone on the team (or a user) suggests a feature beyond MVP scope:

1. Add it to the table above
2. Note who raised it and why it's interesting
3. Explain briefly why it's deferred
4. Move on -- don't design it, don't discuss implementation details

content.content:
1→# Domain Glossary
     2→
     3→Maps business terms to Rust types. This is the ubiquitous language of our todo list
     4→application. Code should read like these definitions -- if the business says "complete
     5→a todo," the code says `complete_todo()`.
     6→
     7→Maintained by the mob. Changes reviewed by the domain architect (Scott Wlaschin).
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## Core Domain Types
    12→
    13→| Domain Term | Rust Type | Module | Notes |
    14→|-------------|-----------|--------|-------|
    15→| User | `User` | `domain` | An authenticated user with valid credentials |
    16→| User ID | `UserId(Uuid)` | `domain` | Newtype wrapper; uniquely identifies a user |
    17→| Email address | `ValidatedEmail` | `domain` | Constructed via `ValidatedEmail::parse()`; validated format, not arbitrary string |
    18→| Password | (not stored as domain type) | -- | Hashed with Argon2 at the boundary; the domain never sees raw passwords |
    19→| Todo item | `TodoItem` (enum) | `domain` | Sum type: `Pending` or `Completed` variant. No boolean flags. |
    20→| Todo item ID | `TodoItemId(Uuid)` | `domain` | Newtype wrapper; uniquely identifies a todo item |
    21→| Todo title | `TodoTitle` | `domain` | Non-empty, max length enforced; constructed via `TodoTitle::parse()` |
    22→| Pending todo | `TodoItem::Pending { ... }` | `domain` | A todo that has not been completed; has title and created_at |
    23→| Completed todo | `TodoItem::Completed { ... }` | `domain` | A todo that has been completed; has title, created_at, and completed_at |
    24→
    25→## Domain Actions (Ubiquitous Language)
    26→
    27→| Business Action | Rust Function / Method | Notes |
    28→|-----------------|----------------------|-------|
    29→| Register a user | `register_user()` | Creates a new user account with validated email |
    30→| Log in | `authenticate()` | Verifies credentials, establishes session |
    31→| Log out | `end_session()` | Terminates the user's session |
    32→| Add a todo | `add_todo()` | Creates a `TodoItem::Pending` with a validated title |
    33→| Complete a todo | `complete_todo()` | Transitions `Pending` -> `Completed`; records completion time |
    34→| Delete a todo | `delete_todo()` | Removes a todo item from the user's list |
    35→| Edit a todo title | `edit_todo_title()` | Updates the title on an existing todo item |
    36→
    37→## Domain Errors
    38→
    39→| Error Condition | Rust Type | Notes |
    40→|-----------------|-----------|-------|
    41→| Title is empty | `TodoError::TitleEmpty` | Todo title cannot be blank |
    42→| Title too long | `TodoError::TitleTooLong { max, actual }` | Exceeds maximum allowed length |
    43→| Todo not found | `TodoError::NotFound { id }` | Referenced todo does not exist |
    44→| Email invalid | `AuthError::InvalidEmail` | Email fails format validation |
    45→| Duplicate email | `AuthError::EmailAlreadyExists` | Registration attempted with existing email |
    46→| Invalid credentials | `AuthError::InvalidCredentials` | Login failed (generic -- no info leak) |
    47→
    48→## Type Design Principles
    49→
    50→These principles govern how we model the domain:
    51→
    52→1. **Parse, don't validate**: `TodoTitle::parse(input) -> Result<TodoTitle, TodoError>`
    53→   not `fn is_valid_title(input: &str) -> bool`. Parsing produces a typed value that
    54→   carries proof of validity. Validate once at the boundary; never re-validate inside.
    55→
    56→2. **Make illegal states unrepresentable**: A `TodoItem` is either `Pending` or
    57→   `Completed` -- never both, never neither. The enum enforces this at compile time.
    58→   No boolean `is_completed` flag with an `Option<completed_at>` that could be
    59→   inconsistent.
    60→
    61→3. **Newtypes for everything**: `UserId` is not a bare `Uuid`. `ValidatedEmail` is
    62→   not a bare `String`. The type system prevents mixing them up. These are zero-cost
    63→   abstractions in Rust.
    64→
    65→4. **Pure domain core**: Everything in `src/domain/` is pure functions and types. No
    66→   database imports, no HTTP types, no I/O. If you need a mock to test domain logic,
    67→   I/O has leaked into the domain.
    68→
    69→5. **Workflows as type-safe pipelines**:
    70→   ```
    71→   UnvalidatedInput -> ValidatedInput -> DomainResult
    72→   ```
    73→   Each step's output type is the next step's input. You cannot skip validation.
    74→
    75→## State Machine: TodoItem
    76→
    77→```
    78→                add_todo()
    79→  [nothing] ───────────────> Pending
    80→                                │
    81→                  complete_todo()

# Domain Glossary

Maps business terms to Rust types. This is the ubiquitous language of our todo list
application. Code should read like these definitions -- if the business says "complete
a todo," the code says `complete_todo()`.

Maintained by the mob. Changes reviewed by the domain architect (Scott Wlaschin).

---

## Core Domain Types

| Domain Term | Rust Type | Module | Notes |
|-------------|-----------|--------|-------|
| User | `User` | `domain` | An authenticated user with valid credentials |
| User ID | `UserId(Uuid)` | `domain` | Newtype wrapper; uniquely identifies a user |
| Email address | `ValidatedEmail` | `domain` | Constructed via `ValidatedEmail::parse()`; validated format, not arbitrary string |
| Password | (not stored as domain type) | -- | Hashed with Argon2 at the boundary; the domain never sees raw passwords |
| Todo item | `TodoItem` (enum) | `domain` | Sum type: `Pending` or `Completed` variant. No boolean flags. |
| Todo item ID | `TodoItemId(Uuid)` | `domain` | Newtype wrapper; uniquely identifies a todo item |
| Todo title | `TodoTitle` | `domain` | Non-empty, max length enforced; constructed via `TodoTitle::parse()` |
| Pending todo | `TodoItem::Pending { ... }` | `domain` | A todo that has not been completed; has title and created_at |
| Completed todo | `TodoItem::Completed { ... }` | `domain` | A todo that has been completed; has title, created_at, and completed_at |

## Domain Actions (Ubiquitous Language)

| Business Action | Rust Function / Method | Notes |
|-----------------|----------------------|-------|
| Register a user | `register_user()` | Creates a new user account with validated email |
| Log in | `authenticate()` | Verifies credentials, establishes session |
| Log out | `end_session()` | Terminates the user's session |
| Add a todo | `add_todo()` | Creates a `TodoItem::Pending` with a validated title |
| Complete a todo | `complete_todo()` | Transitions `Pending` -> `Completed`; records completion time |
| Delete a todo | `delete_todo()` | Removes a todo item from the user's list |
| Edit a todo title | `edit_todo_title()` | Updates the title on an existing todo item |

## Domain Errors

| Error Condition | Rust Type | Notes |
|-----------------|-----------|-------|
| Title is empty | `TodoError::TitleEmpty` | Todo title cannot be blank |
| Title too long | `TodoError::TitleTooLong { max, actual }` | Exceeds maximum allowed length |
| Todo not found | `TodoError::NotFound { id }` | Referenced todo does not exist |
| Email invalid | `AuthError::InvalidEmail` | Email fails format validation |
| Duplicate email | `AuthError::EmailAlreadyExists` | Registration attempted with existing email |
| Invalid credentials | `AuthError::InvalidCredentials` | Login failed (generic -- no info leak) |

## Type Design Principles

These principles govern how we model the domain:

1. **Parse, don't validate**: `TodoTitle::parse(input) -> Result<TodoTitle, TodoError>`
   not `fn is_valid_title(input: &str) -> bool`. Parsing produces a typed value that
   carries proof of validity. Validate once at the boundary; never re-validate inside.

2. **Make illegal states unrepresentable**: A `TodoItem` is either `Pending` or
   `Completed` -- never both, never neither. The enum enforces this at compile time.
   No boolean `is_completed` flag with an `Option<completed_at>` that could be
   inconsistent.

3. **Newtypes for everything**: `UserId` is not a bare `Uuid`. `ValidatedEmail` is
   not a bare `String`. The type system prevents mixing them up. These are zero-cost
   abstractions in Rust.

4. **Pure domain core**: Everything in `src/domain/` is pure functions and types. No
   database imports, no HTTP types, no I/O. If you need a mock to test domain logic,
   I/O has leaked into the domain.

5. **Workflows as type-safe pipelines**:
   ```
   UnvalidatedInput -> ValidatedInput -> DomainResult
   ```
   Each step's output type is the next step's input. You cannot skip validation.

## State Machine: TodoItem

```
                add_todo()
  [nothing] ───────────────> Pending
                                │
                  complete_todo()
                                │
                                v
                            Completed
```

Both `Pending` and `Completed` variants can be deleted (`delete_todo()`).
The `edit_todo_title()` action applies to both states.

content.text:
Good work from Marty and Scott. The future-ideas file is exactly right — a clean place to redirect scope creep. And the domain glossary nails the ubiquitous language.

I notice Marty hasn't created user stories yet (no `docs/user-stories.md`). Let me also check the CSS foundation that Lea completed and see what Luca's got so far on the Rust side.

The CSS is clean and well-organized. From a UX perspective, the `.container` at 40rem (640px) is the right call for a todo list — narrow, focused, no wasted space. Good.

The Rust scaffolding is taking shape. The project is progressing well. Now let me be proactive and share my UX thinking with Carson, since the HTMX interaction patterns will make or break the user experience.

input.content:
Carson, Steve Krug here. I want to think through the HTMX interaction patterns from a usability standpoint before we start building features.

**The golden rule: the user should never wonder "did that work?"**

Here's what I think the interactions should feel like:

**Adding a todo (Task 6):**
- User types in the input, presses Enter (or clicks "Add")
- The form submits via HTMX, the new todo appears in the list immediately
- The input clears and keeps focus — ready for the next item
- If the input was empty, nothing happens. No error. Just... nothing. Don't punish someone for accidentally hitting Enter.

**Completing a todo (Task 7):**
- User clicks the checkbox
- HTMX updates the item — strikethrough, muted color, moves to bottom (or stays in place, team decides)
- Instant visual feedback. No loading spinner for a checkbox.

**Deleting a todo (Task 8):**
- User clicks the delete button
- The item disappears from the list (HTMX `outerSwap` or whatever removes the element)
- No "are you sure?" modal. It's a todo item, not a bank transfer. If the team insists on a safety net, make it undo, not confirm.

**Editing a todo (Task 9):**
- User clicks on the todo text (or an edit button)
- The text becomes an editable input (HTMX swaps the display text for an input field)
- User edits, presses Enter or clicks away to save
- Escape cancels without saving
- The text goes back to display mode

**Key principle: progressive enhancement.**
Every one of these should work as a standard HTML form submission without JavaScript. HTMX just makes it feel smoother. If HTMX fails to load, clicking the checkbox still submits a form that reloads the page with the updated state.

**No loading states for fast actions.** A todo list is tiny. The server should respond in milliseconds. Don't show spinners. If something takes long enough to need a spinner, something is wrong.

What's your thinking on the HTMX patterns for these interactions?

content.text:
I've now shared my UX perspective with the key teammates:

- **Marty** (Product): Detailed UX principles for every upcoming feature — registration, login, and the todo list interactions. Minimal friction, plain language, straight to the point.
- **Steve Schoger** (Design): Visual hierarchy guidance — one focal point per screen, empty states that invite action, scannable completed/pending distinction, generous whitespace.
- **Heydon** (Accessibility): Alignment on focus management after HTMX swaps, error message accessibility, real checkboxes with real labels, and inline editing discoverability.
- **Carson** (HTMX): Interaction patterns for every feature — add, complete, delete, edit — with the golden rule that the user should never wonder "did that work?"

The scaffolding (Task 1) and walking skeleton (Task 2) are still in progress. Task 3 (CSS tokens) is done — Lea did solid work. The CSS has a 40rem container which is exactly right for a focused todo list.

My job now is to wait for the feature work to begin (Tasks 4-9) and be ready to review every screen, every label, every error message. I'll be mentally walking through every interaction as if I've never seen the app before.

Let me check if there are any user stories from Marty I should review.
No user stories yet. Let me nudge Marty on that — the team will need them before starting Task 4.

input.content:
Marty, quick follow-up — have you started on the user stories yet? Tasks 1 and 2 are still in progress, but once the walking skeleton is done, the team will need clear acceptance criteria for Task 4 (registration). 

I'd love to review the stories before the mob starts building. My main concern: make sure the acceptance criteria are written from the user's perspective, not the system's. "User sees their todo list after registering" not "System creates session and redirects to /todos." The tests should read like things a real person would say.

No rush, but wanted to make sure we're lined up before features start.

Good. I'm in a holding pattern now, which is fine. The infrastructure work (Tasks 1-2) needs to finish before feature work begins. I've planted the UX seeds with all the right people. When the feature tasks unblock, I'll be in every review making sure we don't make the user think.

Let me also quickly check on Kent — he's the TDD coach, and I want to make sure he knows that test names should read like user behavior, not system behavior.

input.content:
Kent, Steve Krug here. Quick thought on test naming as we get ready for feature work.

I know you're the TDD authority, and I'm just the usability guy, but I care about one thing in the tests: **test names should read like user behavior, not system behavior.**

Good: `adding_a_todo_shows_it_in_the_list`
Good: `completing_a_todo_shows_strikethrough`
Good: `registration_with_existing_email_suggests_signing_in`

Less good: `post_todos_returns_201_with_location_header`
Less good: `create_user_inserts_row_in_users_table`

The first set tells you what the user experiences. The second tells you what the system does. Both are valid tests, but the first set doubles as usability documentation. When someone reads the test suite, they should understand what the app does from the user's point of view.

Just a thought to keep in mind as we write the first feature tests. Happy to defer to your judgment on test structure.
input.summary: Test naming from user behavior perspective
